clues: A serious film or show dealing with emotional, real-life situations.
It explores conflict, relationships, and human struggle on screen.
Think tears, tension, and powerful acting performances.
Movies like Schindler's List or Titanic fall into this category.
Often the genre that dominates awards season and earns Oscar nominations.
The opposite of a lighthearted comedy, it aims to move you deeply.
You know when a movie makes the whole room go quiet and somebody starts crying.
It's the heavy stuff, no jokes, just intense emotional situations.
Teenagers love stirring up real-life ones of these in the schoolyard too.
Think Marriage Story or The Pursuit of Happyness, all feelings and no laughs.
A film or show designed to make you laugh out loud.
Jokes, funny situations, and humorous characters fill this genre.
You might find it on a Friday night to lighten the mood.
Friends and The Office are famous examples of this type.
Stand-up performers build their whole careers on this kind of material.
A rom-com mixes this with a love story to keep you smiling.
You know, the kind of movie where you're snorting and clutching your sides.
It's the opposite of a sad serious film, it just wants you giggling.
Think Jim Carrey pulling faces or Adam Sandler being a goofball.
Where the whole point is the punchlines and the laugh track.
A tense, suspenseful film that keeps you on the edge of your seat.
It often involves danger, chases, or a race against time.
Your heart races and you fear for the main character throughout.
James Bond and Hitchcock movies are classic examples of this.
Spies, kidnappings, and ticking bombs are common plot ingredients here.
Often confused with horror, but it scares through tension rather than monsters.
You know that nail-biting type where you're gripping the couch the whole time.
Think Jason Bourne or a hostage standoff where anything could go wrong.
It's the genre built entirely around making you nervous and breathless.
Where there's always a twist and you can't trust anyone on screen.
A film genre meant to frighten and scare the audience.
Monsters, ghosts, and jump scares are common in these movies.
You might watch it with the lights on and a blanket ready.
Halloween and The Shining are well-known films of this type.
Slashers, haunted houses, and zombies all belong under this umbrella.
Producers love it because it scares up huge profits on tiny budgets.
You know, the kind you watch through your fingers and can't sleep after.
It's what you put on at Halloween to make everyone scream.
Think creepy dolls, masked killers, and something lurking in the dark.
The genre where you yell 'don't go in there!' at the screen.
A genre featuring magical worlds, creatures, and impossible events.
Dragons, wizards, and enchanted kingdoms often appear in this type.
It takes place in a world that could never exist in reality.
Lord of the Rings and Harry Potter belong to this category.
Often paired with sword fights and quests across mythical lands.
Unlike sci-fi, its impossible feats come from magic rather than technology.
You know, elves and spells and made-up worlds with their own maps.
Think Game of Thrones, all wizards and mythical beasts and kingdoms.
It's the genre where someone waves a wand and reality bends.
Where a kid finds out they're secretly a chosen hero with powers.
A genre centered on solving a crime or uncovering a secret.
The audience tries to figure out who did it before the reveal.
Clues, detectives, and hidden truths drive the entire story forward.
Agatha Christie novels are classic examples of this storytelling type.
A whodunit keeps you guessing about the culprit until the final scene.
Sherlock Holmes spends his stories unraveling exactly this kind of puzzle.
You know, the kind where you're trying to guess the killer the whole time.
Think Knives Out or Murder on the Orient Express, full of suspects.
It's the genre where the big reveal explains everything at the end.
Where a detective gathers everyone in a room to name the guilty one.
A genre focused on love stories and relationships between characters.
Two people meet, fall in love, and usually overcome obstacles together.
Valentine's Day movies often belong to this warm and emotional genre.
The Notebook and Pride and Prejudice are famous examples of this.
Roses, candlelit dinners, and heartfelt confessions define this tender genre.
A 'meet-cute' is the charming first encounter that kicks off these stories.
You know, the mushy kind where they finally kiss in the rain at the end.
Think Titanic or any movie where two people fall for each other hard.
It's the genre that makes you sigh and reach for the tissues over love.
Where there's always a big airport run to confess true feelings.
A film that tells the true life story of a real person.
It dramatizes the real events and achievements of a famous figure.
You might learn about a musician, athlete, or historical leader this way.
Films like Bohemian Rhapsody and Lincoln fall into this category.
The credits often end with photos of the actual person it portrays.
Actors winning Oscars for portraying real icons usually star in one of these.
You know, when an actor totally transforms to play a real famous dead guy.
Think the Freddie Mercury movie or Ray about the blind singer.
It's a life story of an actual person turned into a movie.
Where they cast someone who looks just like a real historical legend.
A genre set in the American frontier, usually in the 1800s.
Cowboys, horses, sheriffs, and dusty desert towns are typical here.
Showdowns at high noon and outlaws on horseback fill these stories.
Clint Eastwood and John Wayne are icons of this film genre.
Saloons, gunslingers, and tumbleweeds set the scene for this dusty genre.
Sergio Leone's spaghetti films revived this once-fading American style.
You know, cowboys squinting at each other before a quick-draw duel.
Think Django or any movie with a lone rider and a six-shooter.
It's the genre with cattle, deserts, and a sheriff with a star badge.
Where the hero rides off into the sunset after the final shootout.
A non-fiction film that explores real events, people, or issues.
It uses interviews, footage, and facts to inform the audience.
You might watch one to learn about nature, history, or society.
Planet Earth and Super Size Me are well-known examples of this.
No actors or script here, just real life captured on camera.
Streaming services release true-crime ones that everyone talks about for weeks.
You know, the kind narrated by David Attenborough about animals and oceans.
Think Tiger King or Making a Murderer, all real and no acting.
It's a movie that's basically teaching you something true with footage.
Where talking heads explain real events between clips of actual footage.
A film or show where characters express themselves through songs and dance.
The story advances through performances and choreographed numbers on screen.
Les Miserables and Mamma Mia are popular examples of this genre.
Singing and dancing are as important as the plot in these productions.
Broadway is the live theater home of this song-filled storytelling style.
La La Land and The Sound of Music are beloved screen examples.
You know, when characters randomly burst into song in the middle of talking.
Think Grease or Hamilton, where everyone sings their feelings out loud.
It's the kind of show where a big dance number breaks out on the street.
Where the plot stops so the whole cast can do a choreographed number.
A form of comedy that mocks and criticizes society, politics, or people.
It uses humor and irony to expose the flaws of the powerful.
Shows like South Park and movies like Don't Look Up use this technique.
The goal is to make people laugh while making them think critically.
Saturday Night Live skits often skewer politicians using this sharp approach.
Behind the laughs lies a serious jab at corruption or hypocrisy.
You know, comedy that's actually making fun of the government and society.
Think The Daily Show ripping into the news to expose how dumb it is.
It's funny but it's secretly criticizing politicians and the rich.
Where the joke has a sharp point and is really mocking the powerful.
A humorous imitation of a well-known film, show, or genre.
It exaggerates familiar scenes and characters to get laughs from fans.
Scary Movie makes fun of popular horror films using this technique.
You need to know the original work to fully enjoy the jokes.
Mel Brooks built a career poking fun at other films this way.
Spaceballs is a famous send-up of Star Wars using this comedic style.
You know, a movie that copies a famous one but turns it into a joke.
Think Weird Al doing a silly version of a hit song you already know.
It's a goofy spoof that mocks something everyone's already seen.
Where they recreate famous scenes but make them ridiculous for laughs.
A massive, hugely popular film with a very large budget and audience.
It dominates cinemas worldwide and earns hundreds of millions of dollars.
Avengers and Avatar are prime examples of this kind of release.
Studios spend enormous amounts on special effects and star-studded casts for these.
The term originally described a film with lines wrapping around the city block.
A summer release packed with explosions hopes to become one of these hits.
You know, the giant smash-hit movie everyone's lining up to see opening weekend.
Think Jurassic Park or Titanic, the kind that breaks box-office records.
It's the huge, expensive crowd-pleaser that rakes in a billion dollars.
Where the studio bets a fortune hoping it becomes the hit of the year.
A series of related films or shows sharing the same characters and world.
It expands over many installments and builds a loyal fan base.
Marvel and Star Wars are two of the most famous examples today.
New entries keep getting made because audiences keep coming back for more.
Lunchboxes, toys, and theme-park rides spin off from these mega-properties.
James Bond is one of the longest-running examples in cinema history.
You know, when one movie becomes like ten movies all in the same world.
Think Fast and Furious cranking out part after part for decades.
It's a whole connected universe of films milking the same story.
Where the studio keeps the cash machine going with endless installments.
A fresh restart of an existing series with a new cast and story.
It retells a familiar property from the beginning for a new audience.
The 2005 Batman Begins restarted the entire Batman story from scratch.
Studios do this when a franchise has gone stale or lost momentum.
Spider-Man has had several of these, each erasing the previous continuity.
It wipes the slate clean rather than continuing the old storyline.
You know, when they hit the reset button and start the whole series over.
Think how they keep starting Spider-Man fresh with a brand-new actor.
It's like pressing restart on a tired old film series from scratch.
Where they ignore everything before and begin the story all over again.
A new version of an older film, retelling the exact same story.
Filmmakers update the original with modern actors and production techniques.
The Lion King 2019 is a recent example of this for a classic.
It differs from a sequel because it covers the same events again.
Hollywood often does this to a beloved foreign film for English-speaking audiences.
Psycho got a shot-for-shot one of these in 1998.
You know, when they redo an old classic with shinier modern effects.
Think Disney turning their old cartoons into live-action versions.
It's the same exact story you've seen, just filmed fresh decades later.
Where they update a dusty old film instead of inventing something new.
A new show or film focusing on a side character from an existing story.
It takes one element of a bigger world and builds a new story around it.
Like how that lawyer from Breaking Bad got his own whole prequel series.
Fans enjoy getting more time with characters they already love from before.
Frasier launched from Cheers, following a single bar regular into his own show.
The little yellow henchmen from Despicable Me got their own movies this way.
You know, when a minor character gets popular and earns their own show.
Think how Joey got his own series after being on a famous sitcom.
It's a fresh series built around someone from a show you already know.
It's basically a sequel that follows a supporting cast member instead of the lead.
A film that tells the story of events that happened before the original.
It shows the backstory and origins of characters fans already know.
Rogue One shows events that happened just before the first Star Wars film.
The timeline goes backward, filling in gaps from an existing story.
The Star Wars films numbered I through III all serve this purpose.
It answers the question of how the hero became who they are.
You know, the kind that rewinds to show what happened before it all began.
Think the new Willy Wonka movie showing him young before the chocolate factory.
It's a movie set earlier in the timeline than the one you saw first.
Where you finally see the origin story leading up to the original film.
A film that continues the story where the previous one left off.
It follows the same characters into new adventures and challenges.
Toy Story 2 picks up after the events of the first movie.
Studios make these when a film is successful and audiences want more.
The number 2 in a movie title almost always signals one of these.
The Empire Strikes Back is widely considered a better one than its predecessor.
You know, the follow-up movie that comes out after the first one's a hit.
Think Frozen 2 or any 'part two' that keeps the story rolling forward.
It's what you watch next when you want to know what happens after.
Where the same heroes return for round two of their adventure.
A short, funny TV show with the same characters in recurring situations.
Each episode is self-contained and usually runs about thirty minutes.
Friends and Seinfeld are among the most beloved examples ever made.
A laughtrack or live audience often reacts to the jokes in these shows.
The Big Bang Theory and The Office are streaming staples of this format.
Multi-camera ones are often filmed in front of a live studio crowd.
You know, the half-hour funny TV show with the same gang every week.
Think How I Met Your Mother, the kind with a recorded laugh after jokes.
It's a comedy series set in the same apartment or office every episode.
Where the same characters get into a fresh funny mess each week.
A feeling of tension and anxiety about what will happen next in a story.
The audience knows danger is coming but the character does not yet.
Hitchcock was a master at building this feeling throughout his films.
It keeps viewers glued to the screen, afraid to look away even briefly.
A bomb under the table that only the audience sees creates this exact tension.
Edge-of-your-seat dread that something terrible is about to occur.
You know that knot in your stomach when you just know something bad's coming.
Think the slow creep up the stairs where you're holding your breath.
It's that unbearable wait when the music swells and nobody's moved yet.
Where the longer they delay the scary reveal, the more you squirm.
The overall mood or emotional feel a film or scene creates for the audience.
A director chooses lighting, music, and pacing to establish this quality.
A dark, gritty movie has a very different one compared to a bright comedy.
It sets the emotional expectations before the story even really begins.
It can shift from playful to menacing with just a change in music.
Critics describe a movie as 'dark' or 'whimsical' when discussing this quality.
You know how a film just feels gloomy or upbeat, that whole vibe.
Think how the same scene feels totally different with creepy music versus cheerful.
It's the general atmosphere or feeling a movie gives off overall.
Where you'd say a film is 'light' or 'heavy' to describe its mood.
The use of objects or images to represent deeper ideas in a story.
A red rose might stand for love, or a storm might suggest danger.
Filmmakers and writers use this technique to add layers of meaning.
Viewers who look closely can find hidden messages throughout the work.
A white dove released on screen often quietly represents peace this way.
English teachers love asking students what the green light really means.
You know when something on screen secretly means more than it looks like.
Think how a broken mirror in a movie hints at a shattered life.
It's when a simple object stands in for some big abstract idea.
Where the rain isn't just rain, it's standing for sadness or doom.
A recurring element, image, or idea that appears throughout a story.
It repeats to reinforce a theme or create a sense of connection.
In a film, a specific color or object might reappear in key moments.
Unlike a symbol used once, this keeps showing up across the whole work.
The repeated spinning top in Inception is a perfect example of this device.
Composers create musical ones that play each time a hero appears.
You know how a certain image or tune keeps popping up over and over.
Think the same little melody that plays every time the villain shows up.
It's a repeated detail the filmmaker sprinkles throughout on purpose.
Where a particular object keeps reappearing to tie the whole thing together.
The central idea or message that a story explores throughout its entirety.
Good vs. evil, love, and identity are common examples in film and literature.
It is the deeper meaning beneath the plot and the characters actions.
A great film makes you reflect on this long after the credits roll.
Coming-of-age and the corrupting nature of power are common examples in cinema.
Ask 'what is this story really about?' and the answer is this.
You know, the big underlying point the whole movie is trying to make.
Think how a war film might really be about loss or the cost of revenge.
It's the deeper message hiding under all the plot and action.
Where the answer to 'what's it actually about?' isn't just the storyline.
A secondary storyline that runs alongside the main plot of a story.
It involves supporting characters and adds depth to the overall narrative.
In a film, the hero's romance might be this while they save the world.
It connects to the main story but is not the central focus.
You could cut it out and the main story would still make sense.
The best friend's wedding might be this while the leads chase a treasure.
You know, the little side story running in the background of the big one.
Think how the sidekick's love life unfolds while the hero fights the villain.
It's the extra B-story that's not the main thing but adds flavor.
Where the minor characters get their own little arc on the side.
A very brief appearance by a famous person in a film or show.
The actor is usually well-known but plays a tiny, often uncredited role.
Stan Lee famously appeared this way in nearly every Marvel movie.
Fans enjoy spotting these small surprise appearances throughout a film.
Alfred Hitchcock famously gave himself a tiny one in nearly every film he directed.
Blink and you might miss this surprise walk-on by a celebrity.
You know when a huge star pops up for like two seconds and you gasp.
Think Bill Murray suddenly showing up just to wave and disappear.
It's that quick surprise visit by a celeb who isn't really in the movie.
Where a famous face shows up for one tiny scene just for the fun of it.
A single uninterrupted piece of footage recorded by a camera.
It is the basic building block of any film or video production.
Editors cut together many of these to build a complete scene.
Its length and angle can completely change how a moment feels to viewers.
'Action!' begins one and 'cut!' ends it on a film set.
A 'take' is one attempt at recording this small piece of film.
You know, one continuous bit of footage before they yell to stop.
Think how a director frames just one angle of the camera at a time.
It's a single chunk of filming, the smallest piece they later edit together.
Where a 'long' one runs minutes without ever stopping the camera.
A camera framing that fills the screen with a face or small object.
It emphasizes emotion, detail, or the importance of what is shown.
Directors use it to let audiences see every tiny expression on an actor's face.
Pull back from it and you get a wider view of the surroundings.
The opposite framing is a wide or establishing view of the whole scene.
A teary eye filling the whole frame is a textbook example of this.
You know when the camera gets right up in someone's face on screen.
Think how you can count an actor's eyelashes because they're so big in frame.
It's the tight shot where one face takes up the whole screen.
Where the camera zooms way in so you see every freckle and tear.
A three-legged stand used to keep a camera steady during filming.
It prevents shaky footage by holding the camera at a fixed position.
Photographers and videographers set this up before shooting still or moving images.
Its three legs can be extended or adjusted to change the height.
Folding it up and slinging it over your shoulder makes it portable.
Long-exposure night photography is nearly impossible without this steadying gear.
You know, that three-legged thing you screw your camera onto so it stops shaking.
Think how you'd set up a phone on those little stick legs for a group selfie.
It's the stand with three legs that holds the camera perfectly still.
Where you plant it on the ground so the footage isn't all wobbly.
A camera movement where the lens rotates horizontally from side to side.
It follows a moving subject or reveals a wide landscape slowly.
A film crew might use this to track a car driving down a street.
It keeps the camera in one spot while the view sweeps left or right.
Sweep it across a stadium crowd to capture the whole roaring audience.
Its name is short for 'panorama', hinting at the wide sweep it creates.
You know when the camera slowly swings sideways to show the whole scene.
Think how it glides left to right across a beautiful mountain view.
It's that horizontal swivel of the camera without it actually moving.
Where the camera turns side to side on the spot to follow the action.
A camera movement where the lens rotates vertically up or down.
It can reveal a tall building from base to top in one smooth motion.
Unlike a pan, this moves the view on a vertical axis instead.
Directors use it to show scale or follow someone standing up from a chair.
Point the camera at someone's shoes and slowly reveal their face above.
It pivots on a horizontal axis while the camera body stays put.
You know when the camera slowly cranes upward to show a giant skyscraper.
Think how it climbs from the feet all the way up to a villain's face.
It's the up-and-down version of the camera swinging, not side to side.
Where the camera nods upward to reveal how huge something towers above.
A camera technique that makes a subject appear closer or further away.
The lens adjusts to magnify or shrink what is visible in the frame.
It differs from physically moving the camera toward or away from the subject.
A sudden fast version of this can create a dramatic or comedic effect.
A telephoto lens lets you do this without taking a single step.
Pinch your fingers on a phone screen to do this to a photo.
You know when the camera suddenly rushes in close on a shocked face.
Think how you tap and stretch on your phone to make a picture bigger.
It's the lens trick that pulls something far away right up close.
Where the binoculars-style effect brings a distant subject right to you.
A sequence of drawn panels that plan out each shot of a film.
It works like a comic strip showing what the camera will see and when.
Directors use it to plan action scenes and special effects in advance.
Every major Hollywood production creates one of these before filming begins.
Pixar artists sketch these panels long before any animation begins.
Walt Disney's studio pioneered pinning these drawings to a large board.
You know, those rough sketches that look like a comic of the whole movie.
Think how they doodle each scene as little drawings before they shoot it.
It's the comic-strip plan a director draws to map out every shot.
Where each frame of the movie gets sketched out on paper first.
The entire process of making a film, from start to finished product.
It covers everything from hiring the crew to shooting and editing the movie.
A big-budget one can employ thousands of people over several years.
The word is often used to refer to a film or TV show itself.
This phase is bookended by pre- and post- stages of filmmaking.
Theater companies use the same word for a single staged show.
You know, the whole giant operation of getting a movie actually made.
Think of all the work behind the scenes to crank out one film.
It's the entire machine of making a movie, crew, cameras, the lot.
Where 'pre-' comes before it and 'post-' comes after it in filmmaking.
The process of selecting actors to play the roles in a film or show.
A specialist reviews auditions and helps the director fill each character.
Finding the right actor for the lead role is a crucial part of this process.
A bad choice here can seriously hurt how audiences receive the final film.
A director sometimes picks an unknown over a star to fill a part this way.
Type the word 'couch' before this and you get a famous scandal phrase.
You know, picking which actor gets which part before they shoot anything.
Think how they decide who'll play the hero out of a hundred hopefuls.
It's the whole job of choosing the right faces for every role.
Where a director matches each actor to the character they'll play.
A tryout where an actor performs to win a role in a production.
The director and producer watch to see if the performer fits the part.
Actors prepare monologues or read from the script during this process.
Getting a callback means you did well enough for a second one of these.
Singers face a similar tryout when competing on talent shows like The Voice.
Hopefuls line up nervously, hoping to impress with a single performance.
You know, when an actor reads some lines to try and land the part.
Think the nervous tryout on American Idol where you sing for the judges.
It's the test performance you do hoping to get cast in the show.
Where you give it your best shot in front of the director to win the role.
Background performers who appear in scenes but have no speaking lines.
They fill crowds, streets, and restaurants to make a film look real.
A big battle scene might require hundreds of these non-speaking participants.
They are paid but receive far less attention than the main cast members.
You become one yourself if you ever volunteer for a crowd scene.
They blur into the background so the audience focuses on the stars.
You know, the random people walking by in the background of a scene.
Think all those nameless folks filling the restaurant behind the main couple.
It's the crowd of silent people hired just to make a scene look busy.
Where you wave your arms in a stadium shot but never say a word.
A narration recorded separately and layered on top of the visuals of a film.
A character or narrator speaks while the audience watches the on-screen action.
It is commonly used in documentaries and to share a character's inner thoughts.
The speaker is not seen on screen while their words are being heard.
The opening narration of a nature film is delivered this way atop the visuals.
In Shawshank, Red's calm narration runs all the way through the movie like this.
You know when you hear someone talking but their mouth isn't on screen.
Think how a character's inner thoughts play out loud across the picture.
It's that disembodied talking laid on top of the footage afterward.
That narrator we hear but never see, with words floating above the action.
A person who takes an actor's place on set during lighting and camera setup.
They match the actor's height and appearance to help the crew prepare shots.
The real star does not have to wait around while technical adjustments are made.
They are different from a stunt performer who does dangerous physical actions.
A body double is similar, but this person mainly substitutes during setup, not filming.
While the celebrity relaxes in their trailer, this person occupies their mark.
You know, the placeholder person standing where the star will be while they fix lights.
Think someone the same size as the star holding their spot during setup.
It's the substitute who fills in so the crew can prep the shot.
Where a lookalike takes the star's place until the camera's actually ready.
A voice or character that tells or explains the story to the audience.
They provide context, backstory, or commentary that characters cannot share themselves.
Morgan Freeman is famous for playing this role in many well-known films.
In a documentary, this voice guides you through the entire film.
The voice in a nature documentary explaining the lion hunt fills this role.
In Arrested Development, Ron Howard hilariously contradicts the characters as this voice.
You know, the deep voice that tells you what's happening in the story.
Think the calm guy explaining the whole plot as the movie plays.
It's the one telling the tale, sometimes unseen, guiding you through.
Where a storyteller speaks directly to fill you in on what's going on.
The consistency of details from one shot to the next in a film.
A character's coffee cup must stay in the same hand between cuts.
A dedicated crew member watches for mistakes that would break this rule.
Viewers notice errors here as cups, hair, or clothes change between scenes.
A 'goof' on a movie's trivia page usually points out a break in this.
The supervisor takes detailed photos so clothing and props match across takes.
You know when fans spot a Starbucks cup magically appearing between shots.
Think how a glass is full in one cut and empty the next, that mistake.
It's keeping every little detail matching from one camera angle to the next.
Where a character's hair flips sides between cuts and someone goofed.
The process of cutting and assembling footage into the final version of a film.
An editor selects the best takes and arranges them in the right order.
Pacing, tension, and storytelling all depend heavily on the skill used here.
Without this step, a film would just be hours of raw, unorganized footage.
A 'cut' is named after the physical slicing done in this stage long ago.
This is where a film truly takes its final shape after shooting wraps.
You know, the stitching-together of all the clips into the finished movie.
Think how they trim and rearrange all the footage to make it flow.
It's the post-shoot stage where raw clips become an actual film.
Where someone sits at a screen splicing all the takes into one story.
A rapid sequence of short clips edited together to show passing time or change.
It compresses a long process like training or growing up into a short scene.
The famous training sequence in Rocky is a classic example of this technique.
Music usually plays over it while images cut quickly to show progression.
Rocky climbing the museum steps to triumphant music exemplifies this technique.
A makeover scene compressing hours of shopping into one minute uses this trick.
You know, that quick burst of clips showing someone get buff while music plays.
Think the speedy bit where they pack months of training into one minute.
It's the fast cut-up of scenes showing a big change in a flash.
Where a whole journey flies by in clips set to a pumped-up song.
The dark outline of a figure against a bright background, with no detail visible.
You can see the shape but not the features of the person or object.
Sunsets are a popular backdrop for creating this dramatic visual effect in films.
It creates mystery and mood by hiding the identity of a subject completely.
The bat-symbol against the night sky is essentially a giant one of these.
Cut black paper into a profile and you get a portrait version of this.
You know, just a black shape of someone standing against a bright sunset.
Think how you only see the dark outline, no face, no details at all.
It's the shadowy shape you get when light is behind someone.
Where a figure shows up as pure black against a glowing background.
A filming technique that speeds up very slow processes to show change quickly.
A flower blooming or a city going from day to night can be shown this way.
Many hours or days of footage are compressed into just a few seconds.
Documentaries about nature and cities use this technique very frequently.
Clouds racing across the sky in seconds reveal this sped-up technique.
The opposite of slow motion, it crams hours into mere moments.
You know, that sped-up footage where the sunset happens in two seconds.
Think how a whole day of traffic zips by in a quick blur on screen.
It's the trick where hours of slow stuff fly past in a flash.
Where a flower opens up in seconds even though it really took days.
A scene that interrupts the present story to show a past event.
It reveals background information about a character or explains earlier events.
Filmmakers use visual cues like faded color or wavy edges to signal it.
In The Godfather Part II, scenes alternate between past and present this way.
A ripple effect and harp music often signal we are entering one of these.
Lost was famous for revealing character backstories through these jumps to the past.
You know when the screen goes wavy and suddenly we're seeing the character as a kid.
Think how a movie jumps back in time to show what happened years ago.
It's that sudden rewind to a past memory in the middle of the story.
Where the picture blurs and we cut to something from the character's past.
The written document that contains the full story, dialogue, and action of a film.
Every scene, character line, and stage direction is written out in this format.
Writers spend months or years crafting this before a film goes into production.
Without it, actors and directors would have no official blueprint to follow.
The Academy awards a coveted Oscar for the best original one each year.
Formatted in Courier font, it follows strict industry layout conventions.
You know, the whole movie written out on paper before they film a thing.
Think the thick stack of pages with every line and scene typed out.
It's the writer's blueprint that the entire movie gets built from.
Where the writer plots out all the dialogue and action page by page.
The written text containing all the dialogue and instructions for a production.
Actors study it carefully to memorize their lines and understand their characters.
It serves as the blueprint for every scene filmed during production.
A finished screenplay becomes this once it is actively being used on set.
A 'kit' version is the slimmed-down copy actors keep on the day of shooting.
Stage actors clutch this in early rehearsals before they go 'off-book'.
You know, the pages the actors hold and read their lines off of.
Think the bundle the cast flips through to learn what to say.
It's the written set of lines actors memorize for their roles.
Where the phrase 'flip the' before it means swapping the usual roles around.
Products applied to an actor's face and body to change their appearance.
It can add age, wounds, or color to help an actor fit their character.
A dedicated artist applies this before every shooting day on a film set.
Fantasy films use heavy amounts of it to create monsters and alien creatures.
Foundation, powder, and brushes are tools of the person who handles this on set.
A trailer on location often has a chair and mirror reserved just for this work.
You know, the stuff people put on their face before going out for a night.
Lipstick, mascara, eyeliner, that whole kit you'd find in a purse.
It's that thing you take off at night with a wipe before bed.
Think of a beauty counter at a department store covered in this stuff.
Artificial body parts or appliances applied to an actor to alter their appearance.
They can create realistic scars, noses, ears, or even entire creature faces.
Gary Oldman wore extensive facial ones to transform into Winston Churchill.
Special effects artists spend hours sculpting and applying these before filming.
Latex pieces glued onto skin let an actor look wounded, aged, or inhuman.
The Planet of the Apes films relied heavily on these molded appliances for their ape characters.
It's also the word for a fake leg or arm someone wears after losing a limb.
Think of the zombie's rotting face in a horror movie, all built up out of these.
A dentist might give you a fake replacement tooth, which is technically one of these.
You know those fake bellies and chins they stick on actors to make them look fat or old.
The art and craft of capturing visuals on camera in a film.
It involves choosing lighting, lenses, angles, and camera movement for each scene.
Roger Deakins is one of the most celebrated artists of this discipline today.
A film can have a great story but still feel flat without strong work here.
The Academy Award for Best Achievement in this honors the camera work behind a film.
Emmanuel Lubezki won three Oscars in a row for his mastery of this craft.
It's basically the reason a movie looks gorgeous even with the sound off.
The director of photography is the person in charge of all of this.
When people say a film is visually stunning, this is the craft they're praising.
Think of those sweeping landscape shots and moody lighting, that whole visual language.
The way a director and camera operator compose what is visible in each shot.
It determines where subjects appear within the edges of the screen.
Placing a character off-center can create tension or show their isolation.
Skilled use of this can tell the audience how to feel without a single word.
A wide composition versus a tight close-up are both choices made within this.
Drawing a storyboard helps a crew plan how each shot will be arranged before rolling.
It's also what you do to a photo when you put a border around it on your wall.
You know when a photographer tells you to move so you fit nicely in the picture.
Decide what goes inside the rectangle and what gets cut off, that's this.
Think of how a painter decides where to put the subject inside the canvas edges.
The planned movement and positioning of actors within a scene.
The director instructs performers exactly where to stand, walk, and stop.
It ensures the camera captures the right angles and all actors are in frame.
Rehearsing this before filming helps the crew set up lights and camera positions.
A theater director also plans this before a stage play, mapping every actor's path.
Marking the floor with tape shows performers where to hit their spots during this process.
It's also the word for stopping someone from getting past you in a doorway.
In sports, a lineman does this to keep defenders away from the quarterback.
Choreographing where everyone moves so they don't bump into each other on stage.
Think of mapping out each step a person takes across the set, like a dance plan.
